name: Diplomacy
description: A seven-player board game of pure negotiation set in pre-WWI Europe with no dice, no cards, and no hidden information -- the only variable is each player's ability to persuade, deceive, and betray. Meta's CICERO AI achieved top-10% human performance only by combining a 2.7B-parameter language model with strategic planning, making it the first game where competitive AI required natural language deception.

thumbnail.alt: Diplomacy board showing the map of Europe with army and fleet pieces

media.title: Strategy and Overview for Diplomacy Board Game

sources.title: Human-level play in the game of Diplomacy by combining language models with strategic reasoning
sources.author: Meta Fundamental AI Research Diplomacy Team (FAIR)

sources.title: The Board Game of the Alpha Nerds (Grantland)

time_to_competence.notes: Rules are simple: units support, hold, or move; supply centers determine army counts. A competent game requires understanding basic openings and alliance dynamics, achievable in a few sessions.

time_to_expert.notes: No formal rating system makes this hard to quantify. World Championship winners tend to have 5-15 years of tournament experience. The skill is primarily social rather than computational, resisting structured practice.

feedback_delay.notes: Each game takes 4-8 hours face-to-face. A diplomatic strategy's quality may not be apparent until several turns later. Betrayal timing feedback is especially delayed -- you learn if you stabbed too early only when the game concludes.

failure_cost.notes: No financial stakes in standard play. Social cost can be real -- broken alliances with regular opponents affect future games. The game famously strains friendships.

cognitive_demand.notes: Tactical computation is modest (limited unit counts), but simultaneously modeling seven players' intentions, maintaining multiple deceptive narratives, and tracking alliance networks is deeply demanding.

physical_demand.notes: Seated board game. Face-to-face tournaments involve hours of intense social interaction, which is mentally but not physically taxing.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: No prerequisites beyond basic social skills. The map loosely represents 1901 Europe but no historical knowledge is required.

measurability.notes: No standardized rating system. Tournament scoring systems vary (sum-of-squares, center-count, draw-based). The World Diplomacy Championship exists but results are noisy due to small sample sizes and player-pool variation.

perishability.notes: Social manipulation and strategic thinking skills are relatively durable. Map knowledge and opening theory are stable (the game is unchanged since 1959). Negotiation instincts may dull without practice.

precision_required.notes: Tactical orders are simple and discrete. The precision is in timing: when to betray an ally, when to signal commitment, when to lie. These are judgment calls, not calculations.

ambiguity.notes: Outcomes depend heavily on other players' psychology. The 'correct' move is undefined -- it depends on what six other humans decide to do. Even winning strategies require opponents to cooperate with you.

instruction_dependency.notes: Largely self-taught through play. Written strategy guides exist but the core skill -- social manipulation -- is developed experientially rather than through formal instruction.

environmental_variability.notes: Same map every game, but the human element makes each game radically different. Player personalities, alliances, grudges from previous games, and table dynamics create enormous variability.

concurrent_skills.notes: Simultaneously managing tactical planning, alliance negotiation, deception maintenance, information warfare, emotional regulation, and reading body language/tone across six bilateral conversations per turn.

plateau_density.notes: Difficult to assess due to lack of rating systems. Players report improvement being tied to social maturity and experience with specific opponents rather than studyable techniques.

domain_evolution.notes: Rules unchanged since 1959. No significant metagame evolution. CICERO AI has not meaningfully affected human play strategies. The game is fundamentally static.

observability.notes: Negotiation phases are visible and dramatic -- observers can watch alliances form and shatter. The tactical board state is clear. However, the most skilled play (deception, long-term manipulation) is invisible.

luck_variance.notes: No randomness in mechanics, but outcome variance is high because it depends on other players' decisions. Being assigned a weaker starting power (e.g., Italy) significantly affects win probability.

social_coordination.notes: The entire game IS social coordination. Every action depends on persuading others. No player can win without allies. Success requires managing six simultaneous bilateral relationships across multiple turns.

methodology_notes: Scores informed by the Wikipedia article on Diplomacy, the Meta FAIR CICERO paper published in Science (2022), the Grantland long-form feature on competitive Diplomacy culture, and the International prize list of Diplomacy for tournament history. The high social_coordination and ambiguity scores reflect the game's unique position as a pure negotiation game with no random elements. Confidence is lower than for chess/Go due to the absence of formal rating systems.
